title: Honoring the accomplishments and legacy of César Estrada Chávez.
plain_summary: This bill is a resolution that honors Cesar Estrada Chavez, who was a farmworker that became an important civil rights leader. Chavez started the United Farm Workers of America to help farm workers get fair treatment and better working conditions. The House of Representatives wants to recognize all the good things Chavez did for workers and civil rights. The resolution also encourages all Americans to remember Chavez's legacy and his famous saying "Si se puede," which means "Yes, we can" in Spanish.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.
policy_area: Civil Rights and Liberties, Minority Issues

sections.title: Title
sections.text: This resolution honors the accomplishments and legacy of Cesar Estrada Chavez.

sections.title: Background Information
sections.text: Cesar Estrada Chavez was born on March thirty first, nineteen twenty seven, near Yuma, Arizona. He spent his early years on a family farm. When he was ten years old, the bank took away his family's farm, so Cesar joined thousands of migrant farmworkers who moved from place to place working in fields and vineyards throughout the Southwest. After attending more than thirty elementary and middle schools and finishing eighth grade, Cesar left school to work full time as a farmworker to help support his family. At age seventeen, Cesar joined the United States Navy and served his country with honor for two years. In nineteen forty eight, Cesar returned from military service and married Helen Fabela, whom he had met while working in the vineyards of central California. Cesar and Helen had eight children. As early as nineteen forty nine, Cesar was committed to organizing farmworkers to campaign for safe and fair working conditions, reasonable wages, decent housing, and laws to stop child labor. In nineteen fifty two, Cesar joined the Community Service Organization, an important Latino civil rights group. He worked with this organization to help register people to vote and run campaigns against discrimination in east Los Angeles. Cesar became the national director of the Community Service Organization. In nineteen sixty two, Cesar left the Community Service Organization to start the National Farm Workers Association, which later became the United Farm Workers of America. Cesar strongly believed in the principles of nonviolence practiced by Mahatma Gandhi and Doctor Martin Luther King Junior. Cesar used peaceful tactics including fasting for twenty five days in nineteen sixty eight, twenty five days in nineteen seventy two, and thirty six days in nineteen eighty eight to call attention to the terrible working and living conditions of farmworkers in the United States. Under Cesar's leadership, the United Farm Workers of America organized thousands of migrant farmworkers to fight for fair wages, health care coverage, pension benefits, decent housing, and respect. His union's efforts brought about the passage of the important nineteen seventy five California Agricultural Labor Relations Act, which sought justice and guaranteed certain protections for farmworkers. Through his commitment to nonviolence, Cesar brought dignity and respect to organized farmworkers and became an inspiration and resource for people engaged in human rights struggles throughout the world. Cesar's influence extends far beyond agriculture and provides inspiration for those working to improve human rights, empower workers, and advance the American Dream, which includes all people living in the United States. Cesar died on April twenty third, nineteen ninety three, at age sixty six in San Luis, Arizona, only miles from where he was born. More than fifty thousand people attended Cesar's funeral services in Delano, California. Cesar was buried at the headquarters of the United Farm Workers of America, known as Nuestra Senora de La Paz, located in the Tehachapi Mountains in Keene, California. Since Cesar's death, schools, parks, streets, libraries, and other public facilities, as well as awards and scholarships, have been named in his honor. Ten states and dozens of communities across the United States honor the life and legacy of Cesar on March thirty first of each year. March thirty first is recognized as an official state holiday in California, Colorado, Minnesota, Texas, and Washington, and there is growing support to designate Cesar's birthday as a National Day of Service to memorialize his heroism. During his lifetime, Cesar received the Martin Luther King Junior Peace Prize. On August eighth, nineteen ninety four, Cesar was awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom after his death. On May fifth, two thousand twelve, the Navy christened and launched a dry cargo ship called the USNS Cesar Chavez in honor of Cesar, who served in the Navy during World War Two. On October eighth, two thousand twelve, President Barack Obama authorized the Secretary of the Interior to establish a Cesar Estrada Chavez National Monument in Keene, California. President Barack Obama first proclaimed March thirty first, two thousand ten, to be Cesar Chavez Day and asked all people of the United States to observe March thirty first with service, community, and education programs to honor Cesar's enduring legacy. President Joseph R Biden Junior most recently honored the life and service of Cesar by proclaiming March thirty first, two thousand twenty four, to be Cesar Chavez Day and by asking all people of the United States to observe March thirty first with service, community, and education programs to honor Cesar's enduring legacy. The United States should continue Cesar's efforts to ensure equality, justice, and dignity for all people of the United States.

sections.title: Resolution
sections.text: Therefore, the House of Representatives recognizes the accomplishments and example of a great hero of the United States, Cesar Estrada Chavez. The House pledges to promote the legacy of Cesar Estrada Chavez. The House encourages the people of the United States to commemorate the legacy of Cesar Estrada Chavez and to always remember his great rallying cry Si se puede, which is Spanish for Yes, we can.
